journal-title-group.journal-title: Protein Science : A Publication of the Protein Society

title-group.article-title: AcrIIA8 is a putative phage structural protein of the HTJ2 family that does not inhibit Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9

article-meta.aff: 1 Research Institute of Agriculture and Life Sciences, Seoul National University, Seoul, Korea
2 Advanced Analysis Data Center, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Seoul, Korea
3 Biopharmaceutical Research Center, Korea Basic Science Institute, Ochang, Korea
4 Department of Agricultural Biotechnology, Seoul National University, Seoul, Korea

fn.p: Correspondence , Jeong‐Yong Suh, Department of Agricultural Biotechnology, Seoul National University, Seoul 08826, Korea. Email: jysuh@snu.ac.kr

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Protein Science published by Wiley Periodicals LLC on behalf of The Protein Society.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Anti‐CRISPR (Acr) proteins are phage‐encoded anti‐defense factors that suppress CRISPR‐Cas immunity in bacteria. AcrIIA8 was previously identified as an inhibitor of Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9 (SpyCas9) through functional assays of metagenomic libraries. Here, we report that AcrIIA8 does not inhibit SpyCas9 in biochemical assays under a range of buffer conditions and temperatures. The solution structure and dynamics of AcrIIA8 reveal a six‐stranded β‐barrel fold with flexible β1–β2 and β2–β3 loops, characteristic of phage virion‐assembly proteins. In addition, genomic context analysis places AcrIIA8 and its homologs within conserved prophage morphogenetic regions at the position expected for type II head‐tail joining (HTJ2) proteins. We further detected no interaction between AcrIIA8 and SpyCas9 in NMR titration experiments, suggesting that they do not specifically associate. Taken together, these findings argue against assigning AcrIIA8 as a SpyCas9 inhibitor and instead support its annotation as a putative phage structural protein of the HTJ2 family.

sec.p: Revised 2026 Apr 26; Received 2026 Feb 12; Accepted 2026 May 15; Collection date 2026 Jul.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Bacteria and bacteriophages have been engaged in an evolutionary arms race for defense and counter‐defense. Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR) and CRISPR‐associated (Cas) proteins are the bacterial defense system against invading phages (Barrangou et al., 2007; Brouns et al., 2008; Garneau et al., 2010). The type II CRISPR‐Cas system utilizes a single Cas9 effector protein that associates with crRNA and trans‐activating crRNA (tracrRNA) for target DNA recognition and interference. Bacteriophages and mobile genetic elements produce anti‐CRISPR (Acr) proteins as a countermeasure against the CRISPR‐Cas system (Bondy‐Denomy et al., 2013; Jia & Patel, 2021). AcrIIA8 was identified from metagenomic libraries using functional assays with Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9 (Uribe et al., 2019a). Intriguingly, purified AcrIIA8 did not interact with S. pyogenes Cas9 (hereafter, SpyCas9) but inhibited the nuclease activity of SpyCas9 in the previous biochemical assay.

In this study, we revisited this puzzling observation and investigated the structure and activity of AcrIIA8. We carefully repeated biochemical assays under various conditions to examine the inhibitory activity of AcrIIA8 but failed to reproduce its inhibition of SpyCas9. We then determined the solution structure of AcrIIA8 to explore its functional implications by comparing it with structurally similar proteins. AcrIIA8 adopts a six‐stranded β‐barrel fold characteristic of phage‐encoded head‐tail joining proteins, or connector proteins, involved in virion assembly (Cardarelli et al., 2010; Casjens et al., 2022). Notably, AcrIIA8 is encoded within conserved prophage morphogenetic regions and occupies the genomic position expected for type II head‐tail joining (HTJ2) proteins, further supporting this functional assignment (Figure S1). A search for homologous structures did not suggest potential enzymatic activity of AcrIIA8 responsible for SpyCas9 inhibition. Together, these observations argue against assigning AcrIIA8 as a SpyCas9 inhibitor and instead support its interpretation as a phage structural protein.

sec.title: AcrIIA8 lacks inhibitory activity against SpyCas9
sec.p: AcrIIA8 (a.a. 1–105) is a neutral protein with a theoretical pI of 6.8, and eluted as a monomer in the size‐exclusion chromatography (Figure 1a). We examined the inhibitory activity of AcrIIA8 against SpyCas9, using AcrIIA5 and AcrIF7 as positive and negative controls, respectively. SpyCas9 with single‐guide RNA (sgRNA) effectively cleaved linearized target DNA, which was blocked by AcrIIA5. However, AcrIIA8 did not exhibit any inhibitory activity against SpyCas9 at a 10‐fold excess molar ratio in our biochemical assay (Figure 1b). AcrIIA8 previously showed weak yet noticeable inhibition of SpyCas9 in an in vitro cleavage assay (Uribe et al., 2019a). We then changed the assay buffer from a Tris–HCl‐based buffer (this study) to a HEPES‐based buffer (previous report) to reproduce the original result, but AcrIIA8 did not inhibit SpyCas9 (Figure 1c). Previously, AcrIIA2 completely inhibited SpyCas9 at 22°C but was less potent at 37°C due to its low thermal stability (Jiang et al., 2019). We repeated the inhibition assays at 22°C but failed to detect any inhibitory activity (Figure 1b,c, right). Under all conditions, the positive control AcrIIA5 potently inhibited SpyCas9 nuclease activity.

caption.p: Size‐exclusion chromatogram of AcrIIA8, and nuclease activity assays of SpyCas9 at varying buffers and temperatures. (a) The size‐exclusion chromatogram of AcrIIA8. The elution volumes of standard proteins are shown on top of the chromatogram. The assays were performed with 0.1 μM SpyCas9 and 1 μM of Acr proteins in (b) 20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 150 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 5% glycerol, and 1 mM DTT, or (c) 20 mM HEPES, pH 7.5, 75 mM KCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 10% glycerol, and 1 mM DTT. In parts (b) and (c), the reactions were incubated at 37°C (left panel) and 22°C (right panel).

sec.p: Next, we tested higher concentrations of AcrIIA8 for SpyCas9 inhibition, up to 50 μM (500‐fold excess over SpyCas9), but none showed inhibitory activity at 37°C or 22°C (Figure 2a,b). We note that mixing AcrIIA8 with apo‐SpyCas9 and then with sgRNA did not change the results, indicating that AcrIIA8 does not compete with sgRNA for SpyCas9 binding. Lastly, we measured time‐course activities of SpyCas9 in the presence and absence of AcrIIA8. This experimental setup is based on the idea that a weak AcrIIA8 activity inhibiting SpyCas9 may be detected at an early stage of the reaction, before the target DNA is completely cleaved at the end of the measurement. We pre‐mixed SpyCas9 with AcrIIA8, added sgRNA and target DNA, and quenched the cleavage reaction after 30 s, 1, 5, and 15 min. The time‐course measurement confirmed that AcrIIA8 did not exhibit any detectable inhibitory activity against SpyCas9 over the course of the measurement (Figure 2c). To understand the lack of SpyCas9 inhibition of AcrIIA8, we determined the solution structure of AcrIIA8 and investigated its physical interaction with SpyCas9.

caption.p: Nuclease activity assays of SpyCas9 at varying AcrIIA8 concentrations and reaction times. Cleavage reactions were examined with 0.1 μM SpyCas9 and AcrIIA8 (0.1, 1, 10, 50 μM) in 20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 150 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 5% glycerol, and 1 mM DTT at (a) 22°C and (b) 37°C. (c) Time course analysis of the DNA cleavage by SpyCas9 in the absence and presence of 50 μM AcrIIA8 after 30 s, 1, 5, and 15 min of the reaction.

sec.title: AcrIIA8 adopts a six‐stranded β‐barrel structure
sec.p: The 2D HSQC spectrum of 15N‐labeled AcrIIA8 showed well‐resolved backbone amide resonances, and the wide dispersion of amide resonances suggested a β‐sheet structure (Figure S2). We assigned the chemical shifts of backbone and side chain 1H, 15N, and 13C resonances using a suite of triple‐resonance heteronuclear correlation NMR experiments. Interproton distance restraints were obtained from three‐dimensional 13C‐separated NOESY and 15N‐separated NOESY experiments, and the residual dipolar couplings (RDCs) were measured in 12 mg/mL of pf1 phage alignment medium. We determined the structure of AcrIIA8 based on 1688 NMR restraints comprising 1336 experimental NOE restraints, 226 dihedral angle restraints, 94 backbone 1 D NH RDC restraints, and 32 hydrogen bonding restraints (Table 1).

caption.p: Restraints and structural statistics of AcrIIA8.

fn.p: For the ensemble of the final 20 simulated annealing structures.
The magnitudes of the axial and rhombic components of the alignment tensor were −5.0 ± 0.1 Hz and 0.31 ± 0.04, respectively.

sec.p: AcrIIA8 formed a β‐barrel structure of six β strands, which contained two long loops between β1 and β2 strands and between β2 and β3 strands (Figure 3a). Secondary structures were well‐defined in the 20 lowest‐energy NMR structures, and the β1−β2 and β2−β3 loops exhibited conformational heterogeneity (Figure 3b). A search for structural homologs of AcrIIA8 using the Foldseek program returned two hits with expectation values below 0.01: the gp11 connector protein at the neck of the Myoviridae cyanophage Pam3 virion (PDB code 8HDR) (Yang et al., 2023) and the gp16 stopper protein of bacteriophage SPP1 (PDB code 7Z4W) (Orlov et al., 2022). Pairwise structural alignments of AcrIIA8 with the structural homologs produced Cα root‐mean‐square deviations of 4.27 Å (79 atoms) and 4.14 Å (60 atoms) for gp11 and gp16, respectively (Figure 3c,d). The topology of the β‐barrel core structure was very similar between the homologs, except for the β1−β2 and β2−β3 loop regions. Another search using the DALI program returned several phage‐encoded structural proteins that similarly assemble the neck part of virions. The gp16 stopper protein of the phage SPP1 (PDB code 7Z4W) ranked the top with a Z score of 6.5 and an alignment length of 89 (Table S1).

caption.p: Structure and dynamics of AcrIIA8. (a) The solution structure of AcrIIA8 is shown in a cartoon diagram and a rainbow color scheme. (b) Superimposed 20 lowest‐energy structures of AcrIIA8. Structural alignment of AcrIIA8 with (c) gp11 of the Myoviridae cyanophage Pam3 virion (PDB code 8HDR), and (d) gp16 of bacteriophage SPP1 (PDB code 7Z4W). (e) 1H–15N heteronuclear NOE data of AcrIIA8. A dashed line denotes the heteronuclear NOE value of 0.8, and secondary structures are shown above the NOE data.

sec.p: We obtained 1H–15N heteronuclear NOE data for backbone amide groups to examine whether the β1−β2 and β2−β3 loops exhibit conformational dynamics. The two loop regions were highly dynamic in solution, as indicated by decreased NOE values below 0.8 (Figure 3e). Previously, gp16 of phage SPP1 also showed highly dynamic β1−β2 and β2−β3 loops in solution, which adopted secondary structures upon assembling the SPP1 phage virion (Lhuillier et al., 2009; Orlov et al., 2022). Thus, the structure and loop dynamics of AcrIIA8 closely resemble those of the structural protein gp16 of the phage SPP1 virion.

sec.title: AcrIIA8 does not bind SpyCas9
sec.p: Size‐exclusion chromatography showed that a mixture of 100 μM AcrIIA8 and 20 μM SpyCas9 did not coelute as a complex. We also observed no measurable binding isotherm in isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) experiments, in which 100 μM AcrIIA8 was titrated into 10 μM SpyCas9. These results do not support high‐affinity binding between AcrIIA8 and SpyCas9 with a K D below 1 μM and are consistent with previous biolayer interferometry measurements that failed to detect a binding isotherm between AcrIIA8 and SpyCas9 (Uribe et al., 2019a, 2019b). We note that a published correction to the original paper stated that the reported binding between AcrIIA8 and SpyCas9 could not be reproduced and was attributable to contamination of the original AcrIIA8 preparation with AcrIIA9 (Uribe et al., 2019a, 2019b). To examine a potential weak binding, we performed NMR titration experiments using 100 μM each of AcrIIA8 and SpyCas9. 2D HSQC NMR spectra of 15N‐AcrIIA8 did not show significant line broadening or chemical shift perturbations in the presence of apo‐SpyCas9 or holo‐SpyCas9, indicating that AcrIIA8 does not specifically interact with SpyCas9 (K D >100 μM) (Figure 4).

caption.p: 2D 1H–15N HSQC spectra of AcrIIA8. (a) 100 μM AcrIIA8 in the absence (black) and in the presence (red) of 20 μM apo‐Cas9. (b) 100 μM AcrIIA8 in the absence (black) and in the presence (red) of 30 μM holo‐Cas9.

sec.p: We also modeled the SpyCas9–sgRNA–AcrIIA8 complex using the AlphaFold 3 server. Although the pLDDT scores indicated reasonable confidence for individual chains, the large predicted aligned errors (PAEs) suggested substantial uncertainty in the placement of AcrIIA8 relative to SpyCas9–sgRNA (Figure S3A,B). In the predicted models, AcrIIA8 was positioned between the REC lobe and the C‐terminal domain of SpyCas9, but its binding pose did not converge across the five models (Figure S3C,D).

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS AND PERSPECTIVES
sec.p: Previously, type VI‐A Acr proteins (AcrVIA1–7) were identified through functional and bioinformatic analyses, but their anti‐CRISPR activity was later called into question by independent studies (Johnson et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2020). Similarly, AcrIIA7, which was identified in the same metagenomic screen as AcrIIA8, did not inhibit SpyCas9 but instead suppressed unrelated bacterial defense systems, namely Thoeris and the cyclic‐oligonucleotide‐based anti‐phage signaling systems (CBASS) (Li et al., 2024; Uribe et al., 2019a, 2019b). In this study, AcrIIA8 closely resembled a virion structural protein of the HTJ2 family and showed no apparent inhibitory activity against SpyCas9. Accordingly, its designation as a bona fide anti‐CRISPR protein remains uncertain and should be considered provisional until further validation is available.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Eun‐Hee Kim: Methodology. Jeong‐Yong Suh: Conceptualization; funding acquisition; writing – original draft; writing – review and editing; investigation; supervision. Iktae Kim: Investigation; methodology; writing – review and editing. So Young An: Conceptualization; writing – review and editing; investigation; methodology. Sung‐Hyun Hong: Methodology.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

caption.p: Table S1. Summary of the structural hits from the search on the DALI server using the AcrIIA8 structure as a query. Structures with Z scores above 5 are selected.

Figure S1. Genomic context and sequence conservation of AcrIIA8 and related homologs. (A) Synteny analysis of AcrIIA8 and representative homologs shows that these genes are consistently located within conserved prophage morphogenetic regions containing genes involved in DNA packaging, head assembly, head‐tail joining, tail morphogenesis, and receptor‐binding functions. The AcrIIA8 locus in Clostridium innocuum SFAD8 encodes a protein identical to AcrIIA8 and occupies the genomic position expected for a type II head‐tail joining protein (HTJ2). Gray shading indicates amino acid sequence similarity between homologous proteins. (B) Multiple‐sequence alignment of AcrIIA8 and representative HTJ2 homologs showing strong sequence conservation across the family.
Figure S2. 2D 1H–15N HSQC spectra of AcrIIA8 annotated with the residue names and numbers.

Figure S3. AlphaFold 3 models of SpyCas9‐sgRNA and AcrIIA8. (A) Model 1 of the SpyCas9‐sgRNA‐AcrIIA8 complex, colored by pLDDT score. (B) Predicted aligned error (PAE) plot and chain ipTM scores (mean ± standard deviation). (C) Model 1 of the SpyCas9‐sgRNA‐AcrIIA8 complex, colored by SpyCas9 domains. (D) Structural alignment of five predicted models, with AcrIIA8 shown in different colors.

sec.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
sec.p: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea grants funded by the Korea government (MSTI) (RS‐2025‐23525174 and RS‐2024‐00440614), and the Korea Institute of Marine Science & Technology Promotion (KIMST) funded by the Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries (BDB‐2025‐04‐04230007). We thank the high‐field NMR facility at the Korea Basic Science Institute and the National Center for Inter‐University Research Facilities at Seoul National University.

An SY, Kim I, Hong S‐H, Kim E‐H, Suh J‐Y. AcrIIA8 is a putative phage structural protein of the HTJ2 family that does not inhibit Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9. Protein Science. 2026;35(7):e70651. 10.1002/pro.70651

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The atomic coordinates of the solution structure of AcrIIA8 and the NMR restraints have been deposited in the Protein Data Bank and the Biological Magnetic Resonance Bank under the accession codes 9X91 and 36800, respectively.

caption.p: Table S1. Summary of the structural hits from the search on the DALI server using the AcrIIA8 structure as a query. Structures with Z scores above 5 are selected.

Figure S1. Genomic context and sequence conservation of AcrIIA8 and related homologs. (A) Synteny analysis of AcrIIA8 and representative homologs shows that these genes are consistently located within conserved prophage morphogenetic regions containing genes involved in DNA packaging, head assembly, head‐tail joining, tail morphogenesis, and receptor‐binding functions. The AcrIIA8 locus in Clostridium innocuum SFAD8 encodes a protein identical to AcrIIA8 and occupies the genomic position expected for a type II head‐tail joining protein (HTJ2). Gray shading indicates amino acid sequence similarity between homologous proteins. (B) Multiple‐sequence alignment of AcrIIA8 and representative HTJ2 homologs showing strong sequence conservation across the family.
Figure S2. 2D 1H–15N HSQC spectra of AcrIIA8 annotated with the residue names and numbers.

Figure S3. AlphaFold 3 models of SpyCas9‐sgRNA and AcrIIA8. (A) Model 1 of the SpyCas9‐sgRNA‐AcrIIA8 complex, colored by pLDDT score. (B) Predicted aligned error (PAE) plot and chain ipTM scores (mean ± standard deviation). (C) Model 1 of the SpyCas9‐sgRNA‐AcrIIA8 complex, colored by SpyCas9 domains. (D) Structural alignment of five predicted models, with AcrIIA8 shown in different colors.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The atomic coordinates of the solution structure of AcrIIA8 and the NMR restraints have been deposited in the Protein Data Bank and the Biological Magnetic Resonance Bank under the accession codes 9X91 and 36800, respectively.
